title: Gmail vs HEY for Students
meta_description: Gmail vs HEY for students who need a free inbox for school. See why Gmail wins when you want to create an email account quickly without a subscription.
constraint_lens: Students prefer tools that are free to start and easy to stop using later without paying ongoing fees.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for students who need a free email account quickly for school sign ups.
one_second_verdict.reason: HEY fails first because using the inbox requires paying a recurring subscription instead of creating a free account immediately.

verdict.summary: Gmail is the better choice for students who need a quick inbox for school sign ups. It allows anyone to create a free account and start sending and receiving messages immediately. HEY requires paying a monthly subscription to use the inbox. That requirement adds unnecessary commitment for students who may only need the email account for a semester or short school period.
verdict.decision_rule: If using the inbox requires paying a recurring subscription instead of creating a free account immediately, HEY fails first.

sections.heading: Why Gmail fits Students better
sections.content: Gmail fits this student because HEY is the tool putting a subscription in front of basic email access, not Gmail. That adds a payment decision before the inbox is even useful, makes daily access feel tied to ongoing cost, and raises the switching risk for users who just need dependable email. Gmail wins by making the inbox usable before billing becomes part of the workflow.

bullets.point: Gmail lets the user start sending and receiving email without a billing decision first
bullets.why_it_matters: The inbox works immediately instead of making a subscription the first requirement.

bullets.point: Gmail keeps daily email access available without recurring cost pressure
bullets.why_it_matters: That matters when the user needs reliable email but does not want each month of use to require justification.

bullets.point: Gmail reduces the commitment risk of adopting the tool
bullets.why_it_matters: The user can use the inbox for school or temporary needs without carrying a paid workflow they may not keep.

bullets.point: HEY can still be better when faster power-user email handling is worth paying for
bullets.why_it_matters: The subscription may make sense once the interface is doing enough work to justify its cost.

bullets.point: HEY often offers workflow gains that matter for heavier inbox volume
bullets.why_it_matters: That matters when email speed is a professional bottleneck instead of a basic utility.

bullets.point: HEY may fit once the user wants a premium email workflow rather than a free baseline account
bullets.why_it_matters: The recurring fee only makes sense when the upgrade is clearly part of the job.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: Gmail becomes too basic when the user now needs a premium email workflow badly enough to justify paying for it.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose HEY if the paid interface is now doing real work.

items.fails_when: HEY breaks down when the user needs ordinary email access but does not want a recurring bill standing in front of it.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose Gmail when free access is the actual requirement.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: This can flip if the user now values a premium inbox workflow enough to justify paying for it each month. Then HEY may be worth the subscription.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Choose Gmail if email access should work before any subscription decision.
Choose HEY if a paid inbox workflow is now worth the cost.
Avoid HEY when recurring price is bigger than the email job itself.

faqs.q: Why can free access matter so much?
faqs.a: Because it affects adoption cost, day-to-day access, and how easy it is to keep or drop the tool later.

faqs.q: When is HEY still the better fit?
faqs.a: HEY is still the better fit when its premium workflow is doing enough work to justify the recurring price.

faqs.q: What usually makes HEY fail first?
faqs.a: The user needs ordinary email access but does not want a subscription standing in front of it.
No. It is about whether the paid layer matches the job.
